coredata.dc:title: SnO2/graphene quantum dots composited photocatalyst for efficient nitric oxide oxidation under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Yang
Yu, Shan
Zhong, Yunqian
Zhang, Qian
Zhou, Ying
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, we have prepared tin oxide (SnO2)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composites and applied them for photocatalytic removal of nitric oxide (NO). In contrast to SnO2 alone, SnO2/GQDs composite has exhibited a remarkably enhanced activity under both full spectrum and visible light illumination. The crystal structure, morphology and surface state of the composite were studied by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Moreover, diffraction and reflectance spectra and photoluminescence spectra together with the photoelectrochemical tests show that the presence of GQDs in the composite could promote the visible light response as well as charge separation efficiency of the system. This makes SnO2/GQDs composite generate more active species (O2 − and OH) for NO oxidation, as evidenced from the electron paramagnetic resonance measurements. This study hence could be referenced for future construction of efficient photocatalytic systems with wide bandgap oxides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SnO2
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalytic oxidation of NO
Visible light response
Charge separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism for efficiency enhancement in SnO2/GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21403172 ), the Innovative Research Team of Sichuan Province ( 2016TD0011 ), Sichuan Provincial Education Department Project ( 15ZB0055 ), the Scientific Research Starting Project of SWPU ( 2015QHZ018 ) and Young Scholars Development Fund of SWPU ( 201499010096 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) Photocatalytic removal ratio of NO in the presence of SnO2 and SnO2/GDQs under full spectrum irradiation and visible light irradiation (≥420 nm), respectively. (c) and (d) Photocatalytic recycling tests of SnO2/GQDs (1%) under full spectrum irradiation and visible light irradiation (≥420 nm), respectively.
(a) FT-IR spectra of GQDs, SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%) composite, high-resolution XPS spectra of Sn 3d (b), C 1 s (c), and O 1s (d) of SnO2/GQDs (1%) composite.

(a) Diffuse and reflectance spectra of SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs composites. Inset is the absorption spectrum of GQDs dispersed in water. (b) Photoluminescence spectra of SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs composites. Excitation wavelength: 260 nm. (c) Transient photocurrent response and (d) EIS curves of SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%) under visible light illumination and in darkness.

Band structures of SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%): (a) XPS valence band spectra; (b) band structure diagram. DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra of SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%) in methanol dispersion for DMPO-O2 − (c) and in aqueous dispersion for DMPO-OH (d) with or without visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic NO removal process in SnO2/GQDs composite.

head.ce:title: SnO2/graphene quantum dots composited photocatalyst for efficient nitric oxide oxidation under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Center of New Energy Materials and Technology, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Center of New Energy Materials and Technology
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: The Center of New Energy Materials and Technology, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Center of New Energy Materials and Technology
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. Xie and S. Yu contributed equally in this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SnO2/GQDs has exhibited a remarkable efficiency towards photocatalytic removal of NO as a result of the improved light harvesting ability and enhanced charge separation efficiency with the introduction of GQDs.

In the present work, we have prepared tin oxide (SnO2)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composites and applied them for photocatalytic removal of nitric oxide (NO). In contrast to SnO2 alone, SnO2/GQDs composite has exhibited a remarkably enhanced activity under both full spectrum and visible light illumination. The crystal structure, morphology and surface state of the composite were studied by X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Moreover, diffraction and reflectance spectra and photoluminescence spectra together with the photoelectrochemical tests show that the presence of GQDs in the composite could promote the visible light response as well as charge separation efficiency of the system. This makes SnO2/GQDs composite generate more active species (O2 − and OH) for NO oxidation, as evidenced from the electron paramagnetic resonance measurements. This study hence could be referenced for future construction of efficient photocatalytic systems with wide bandgap oxides.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitric oxide (NO) is a typical component in air pollutant in some of the countries around the world and the removal of nitric oxide is of great importance in consideration of the air purification [1,2]. Various techniques including physical/chemical adsorption [3,4], selective catalytic reduction [5,6], and thermal catalytic reduction [7,8] have been developed for the removal of NO. However, these methods require additional conditions such as high temperature, reducing agents et al., making the removal of NO difficult at the sub-ppm level at ambient temperature. Photocatalytic removal of NO with low concentration (ppb level) is considered as one of the most promising strategies, as it could take advantage of solar light directly without introduction of additional energy source. In the past few decades, various semiconductors such as TiO2 [9], Bi2O2CO3 [10,11], Bi2WO6 [12], C3N4 [13,14] and Bi2O3 [15] have been explored for the photocatalytic NO oxidation and they exhibited attractive activities. However, many of these semiconductors could not oxidize NO completely into nitrate NO3 −, and nitrogen dioxide NO2 was often obtained instead, which could be even more toxic in contrast to NO.

Tin dioxide (SnO2), an n-type semiconductor with the bulk band gap (∼3.60 eV), high stability, excellent electronic and optical properties, are widely used in gas sensor, photoelectrochemistry and photodetector [16–18]. Compared to TiO2, SnO2 has a more positive valence band and hence stronger oxidation ability, which could oxidize the pollutants more completely [19]. Nevertheless, the application of SnO2 in photocatalysis is quite limited as a result of its intrinsic wide bandgap and hence ineffective utilization of visible light [20–22]. This could be solved by modification of the surface state of SnO2 like doping and introduction of oxygen vacancies. [23–26]. For example, our previous work showed that the iodination on the surface of SnO2 can significantly enhance its visible light response due to the formation of band tail states [27].

In addition, the enhancement of visible light response of the system could be achieved after the wide bandgap semiconductors are combined with other narrower bandgap materials. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with unique properties of graphene has drawn a lot of attention [28]. Specifically, owing to the size-resulted quantum confinement and strong edge effects, GQDs have exhibited remarkable visible light absorption, unique electron reservoir ability and photostability [29,30]. GQDs could not only render the visible light response of the system, but also benefit the charge separation efficiency of the system. A series of GQDs modified semiconductor photocatalysts has been reported with enhanced photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical performances, such as TiO2/GQDs [31], CdS/GQDs [32], ZnO/GQDs [33], g-C3N4/GQDs [34], N-doped Bi2O2CO3/GQDs [35] and so on. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, the influence of modification of SnO2 with GQDs on the photocatalytic behavior of SnO2 for NO removal is not yet studied.

In this work, we have successfully coupled ultrasmall SnO2 nanoparticle (∼2.4 nm) with GQDs through a facile method. The photocatalytic behavior of as-prepared SnO2/GQDs composite was evaluated by the removal of NO at ppb level. It turns out that the efficiency of SnO2 could be remarkably improved after the introduction of GQDs with a weight percentage of 1%, meanwhile the high selectivity of the oxidative products towards NO3 − originated from SnO2 maintained. Though the small amount of GQDs in the composite has no obvious influence on the morphology and structure of SnO2, it does enhance the visible light response and charge separation efficiency of the system.

All the chemicals were used as received without further purification and purchased from Chengdu Kelong Co. Ltd. with analytical grade. Ultrapure deionized water (resistivity ≥ 18.25 M) was used in all experiments.

According to the previously reported approach [27], SnO2 were synthesized via hydrolysis of SnI4. Typically, 500 mg of the prepared SnI4 was dispersed into a 100 mL ethanol solution (ethanol: water = 1:4, v/v) under vigorous stirring at 70 °C for 1.5 h. After precipitation, the sediments were collected through centrifugation and washed with ethanol and deionized water several times. Finally, the products (SnO2) were obtained after dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

GQDs were synthesized by an alkali-mediated hydrothermal method [28,31]. 0.75 g of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was dispersed in 150 mL of NaOH (0.2 M) aqueous solution by ultrasonication for 2 h. Next, this suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (250 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooled to room temperature, the product was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the insoluble impurities. Then the solution was purified with dialysis bag (with retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 2 days to remove impurities. Finally, the GQDs powders were obtained by rotary evaporation at 80 °C, which could further form transparent deep-brown solution in water.

Typically, 200 mg of the as-prepared SnO2 was dispersed in 100 mL deionized water followed by ultrasonication for 30 min at room temperature. Then different weight ratio percentage (i.e., 0.5%, 1%, 2% and 4%) of GQDs was added to the suspension, which was kept under vigorous stirring for 5 h, during which the GQDs can homogeneously adsorb on the surface of SnO2. After that, the suspension was evaporated to yield the solid composite. These samples were noted as SnO2/GQDs (X%), where X represents the weight percentage of GQDs in the composites.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of sample were performed on a PANalytical X'pert diffractometer equipped with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were observed by using a FEI Tecnai G2 20 microscope operated at 200 kV. UV–Vis absorption spectra and diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were recorded from the Shimadzu 2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as the reflectance standard sample at ambient temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer with KBr pellets as the substrate. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer with Al Kα emission at 1486.6 eV. Photoluminescence spectra were measured by using Hitachi F-7000 with the light source of MVL-210 (Mejiro Genossen Inc.). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signals of radicals spin-trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1- pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were characterized by JESFA200 spectrometer at room temperature.

Photocatalytic removal of NO at ppb level was detected on our home-made testing system, which has been described in detail before [36]. In our experiment, the flow rate of dry air and NO are 2.0 L min−1 and 12.0 mL min−1 respectively. The removal ratio (η) of NO was calculated by the equation η (%) = [1 − C (NO)/C0] × 100%, where C (NO) is the concentration of NO during the reaction and C0 represents the initial concentration of NO before photocatalytic reaction. Besides, the generation ratio (ω) of NO2 during reaction was calculated by ω (%) = [C (NOx) − C (NO)]/C (NOx) × 100%, where C (NOx) is the concentration of whole nitric oxide.

Photo-electrochemical (PEC) were conducted on a CHI660E electrochemical work station in a conventional three-electrode system with saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and Pt wire as the reference and counter electrode, and Na2SO4 aqueous solution (0.5 M) as the electrolyte. The working electrode was based on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) conducting glass and prepared by doctor-blade method [37]. The corresponding slurry was made from the mixture of deionized water and SnO2 (or SnO2/GQDs). The deposition area was controlled to be 1 cm × 1 cm. For photo-electrochemical measurements, Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300/UV, Beijing Perfect light Technology Co., Ltd.) was used as the light source.

The photocatalytic performances of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by the degradation of air pollutant NO at an indoor ppb level (600 ppb). Before illumination, the samples were exposed to continuous NO gas flow for 30 min to reach the surface adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Under the full spectrum irradiation, all samples show good photocatalytic activity among all the investigated samples (Fig. 1 a). In specific, the activity of the samples increased with a small amount of GQDs (0.5 wt% and 1 wt%), but it dropped with excessive amount of GQDs (2 wt% and 4 wt%). A NO removal ratio of 75% was achieved on SnO2/GQDs (1%). GQDs could improve the activity of SnO2 not only under full spectrum illumination but also under visible light. As shown in Fig. 1b, the removal ratio of NO with bare SnO2 is around 18% within 30 min under visible light illumination. After the deposition of GQDs on SnO2, the NO removal ratio was largely improved. For the composite samples, with the increasing amount of GQDs, the NO removal efficiency was increased significantly. NO removal ratio reaches 57% over the optimized SnO2/GQDs (1%) catalyst after 30 min under visible-light irradiation. A higher or lower amount of GQDs would decrease the activity of the system. Commonly, the incorporation of GQDs in photocatalysts could enhance the absorbance of light and inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs efficiently, thus improve the photocatalytic performance of catalysts [30–35]. However, the light shielding effects from excessive GQDs on the SnO2 surface may be harmful for photocatalysis, which has been reported before and could be evidenced from the UV-is diffuse and reflectance spectra in our present system (see Fig. 3a) [31,32]. Moreover, the excessive GQDs may block the active sites of SnO2, which decreases the photocatalytic efficiency of the system [35].

Comparison of consecutive illumination (Fig. 1a and b) and intermittent illumination (Fig. 1c and d) of the system further confirms that the NO removal from the system is indeed caused from the light illumination. As showed in Fig. 1c and d, after the light was switched on, the concentration of NO in all the reaction systems dropped sharply in the first 5 min and then became steady approximately; when light was switched off, the concentration of NO in the systems went back the the original level immediately. In addition, we also noticed there was a slight loss of photoactivity of the sample after 5 light on-and-off cycles, which might be attributed to the accumulation of degradation product during the long time irradiation [35]. Moreover, NO2 selectivity during the photocatalytic process is also very important because of the secondary pollution from NO2 [38]. Therefore, the formation of intermediates (NO2) in the process of photocatalytic oxidation of NO was monitored as shown in Fig. S1. All the samples exhibited extremely low fraction of NO2 smaller than 5%, indicating that our samples possess stronger oxidation ability, which have promoted the oxidation of NO2 to NO3 −. This demonstrates the outstanding selectivity towards NO3 − for NO removal, which is highly desired. Overall, the SnO2/GQDs (1%) composite material not only shows high photocatalytic activity but also exhibits excellent selectivity for NO oxidation under either full spectrum or visible light illumination.

The XRD patterns of the SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%) are illustrated in Fig. S2. Both the diffraction patterns were well indexed to the tetragonal SnO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1250), and the peaks at 26.6°, 33.9°, 37.9°, 51.7° and 64.7° could be assigned to the (1 1 0), (1 0 1), (2 0 0), (2 1 1) and (1 1 2) facets, respectively. Meanwhile, the significant broadening of the diffraction peaks was ascribed to the very small crystallite size of SnO2 nanoparticles. After the introduction of GQDs, the phase structure of SnO2 was preserved well. However, no characteristic diffraction peaks relevant to GQDs could be observed, which is probably resulted from the low content as well as the casual distribution and relatively low diffraction intensity of GQDs on the surface of SnO2 [32].

The morphologies of the SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%) composite were observed by TEM and HRTEM. The TEM images shows that the prepared SnO2 are tiny particles (approximately 2.4 nm) with uniform size and shape (Fig. S3a), which resembles that of pristine SnO2 as we reported before [27]. A representative HRTEM image in Fig. S3b shows two different lattice fringes with spacings of 0.334 and 0.264 nm, which coincide well with the 2 theta angle of 26.6° and 33.9° in the XRD data and corresponds to the (1 1 0) and (1 0 1) planes of tetragonal phase SnO2, respectively. After coupling SnO2 nanoparticles with GQDs, the TEM image (Fig. S3c) and HRTEM image (Fig. S3d) of resulting SnO2/GQDs (1%) composite shows that the incorporation of GQDs have no obvious change on the morphology, crystal structure and size of SnO2 in the composite. The low content as well as the uniform distribution on the surface of SnO2 resulted in the characteristic morphologies of GQDs cannot be observed.

The existence of GQDs in the SnO2/GQDs and the chemical interaction between the two components were further characterized by FT-IR spectra and XPS. Fig. 2 a showed the FT-IR spectra of the samples. For pristine GQDs, the peaks at 3410, 3135, 1637 and 1401 cm−1 are attributed to the deformation and stretching vibration of OH, and the peak at 1120 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of COC [28]. For SnO2, the broad intense band at 576 cm−1 is the characteristic band of SnO2 attributed to the vibration of SnO, the peaks at 1050 and 1401 cm−1 are assigned to the bending mode of different types of surface hydroxyl groups on SnO2, and the absorption bands at 3410 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 are attributed to OH stretching vibration of the absorbed water in the samples [22]. As for the sample SnO2/GQDs (1%), the above characteristic peaks are discernable in the spectrum of SnO2/GQDs (1%), suggesting that the presence of GQDs on the SnO2.

The chemical compositions of SnO2/GQDs (1%) composites and the interaction between the two components were also studied by XPS analysis. XPS high resolution core level spectra are shown in Fig. 2b–d. The Sn 3d spectrum (Fig. 2b) is composed of one doublet peak centered at 487.3 eV and 495.7 eV, which can be attributed to the binding energies of Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2, respectively, and their splitting energy is 8.4 eV, confirming that the chemical states of the Sn element in the SnO2/GQDs (1%) nanocomposites Sn4+ [39,40]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s can be devolved typically into three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.8 eV attributed to CC, CO and HOCO group, which are mainly generated from GQDs [28,35]. The O 1s fitting spectra (Fig. 2d) exhibit peaks at 530.8 eV, 531.2 eV, 532.2 eV and 533.1 eV, which correspond to the lattice oxygen in SnO2, oxygen defects in the matrix of the metal oxide, surface hydroxyl groups and OC chemical states, respectively [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for efficiency enhancement in SnO2/GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, the efficiency of photocatalytic process is mainly determined by three processes: light absorption, charge separation and migration, and surface reaction [13,15]. Fig. 3 a showed that the as-prepared SnO2 (Fig. 3a black line) has an absorption edge at around 340 nm, corresponding to its band gap of 3.64 eV based on Kubelka-Munk function. After introduction of GQDs, an absorption peak around 472 nm could be observed, which is highly related to the intrinsic absorption of GQDs (inset of Fig. 3a) [35]. The intensity of this peak increases gradually as the amount of GQDs increases from 0.5% to 2%. Therefore, the incorporation of GQDs could effectively improve the light harvesting ability of the composites in the visible light region. However, we noticed that the absorption intensity in the UV light region (220–350 nm) gradually decreased, which indicated that the coverage of GQDs on the surface of SnO2 may block the intrinsic absorption of SnO2.

Furthermore, the charge separation efficiency of bare SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs composite was studied by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. As indicated in Fig. 3b, under an excitation wavelength of 260 nm, pristine SnO2 exhibited typical PL intensity with a broad emission from 280 nm to 485 nm centered at around 380 nm. Introduction of GQDs in the system makes no big influence on the shape of PL peak; however, the corresponding PL intensity has decreased obviously. Such a result suggested that the coupling of GQDs to SnO2 provided an additional charge transfer pathway, hence greatly inhibiting the radiative charge recombination of SnO2. In specific, the PL intensity of SnO2/GQDs composites drops most for SnO2/GQDs (1%), suggesting the radiative charge recombination is suppressed most effectively, which coincide well with its optimized photocatalytic activity.

Photoelectrochemical measurements further demonstrate that the existence of GQDs greatly enhances the photocurrent density of the SnO2/GQDs (1%) in contrast to SnO2 under visible light irradiation (Fig. 3c), which is resulted from the increased visible light response as well as the enhanced charge separation efficiency in the presence of GQDs. Meanwhile, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements have been carried out to study the dynamics for interfacial charge transfer. The diameter of the arc radius of the EIS Nyquist plot of SnO2/GQDs (1%) is much smaller than that of SnO2 no matter it is in the darkness or under light irradiation (Fig. 3d), confirming that GQDs can lead to a much higher charge separation efficiency in the system.

In general, O2−, OH and photogenerated h+ are the main reactive species in photocatalytic reactions [13]. The valence band (VB) of SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%) were investigated through VB XPS spectra (Fig. 4 a) and was calculated to be at 2.98 eV and 3.02 eV, respectively. Combining with the UV–vis diffuse and reflectance spectra (Fig. 3a), the CB minimum of them is −0.66 eV and −0.50 eV, correspondingly (Fig. 4b). According to literature, the standard electrode reduction potential of O2/O2 −, OH−/OH and H2O/OH couples is −0.28 eV, 1.99 eV and 2.37 eV, respectively [36]. Therefore, photogenerated electrons can react with the adsorbed O2 to form O2 − thermodynamically. Meanwhile, photogenerated holes of SnO2/GQDs (+3.02 eV) and SnO2 (+2.98 eV) could oxidize H2O to OH (+2.37 eV). In addition, photogenerated holes could directly oxidize NO to NO3 − as its potential energy is more positive than that of NO2/NO (1.03 eV), HNO2/NO (0.99 eV) and HNO3/NO (0.94 eV) [14]. To confirm these inferences, the EPR spin-trap measurements were carried out with DMPO as the trapping agent. No DMPO-O2 − signals were observed in the darkness for both SnO2 and SnO2/GQDs (1%), whereas signals related to DMPO-O2 − emerged under visible light irradiation (Fig. 4c). Similarly, a typical four-line signal with a relative intensity ratio close to 1:2:2:1 could be observed after light illumination for both samples, which is characteristic of the DMPO-OH (Fig. 4d). Hence, O2 −, OH are indeed existed in the system as the active species for the photocatalytic oxidation of NO. Significantly, signals of O2 − and OH over SnO2/GQDs is much stronger than that of SnO2. This is consistent with the photoelectrochemical test, and it proves that the introduction of GQDs could promote the generation of active species for photocatalysis.

Based on the above experimental data, a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic removal of NO by the SnO2/GQDs composite is presented in Fig. 5 . Under light excitation, electrons and holes are generated from the system, and existence of GQDs could largely increase the amount of photogenerated electrons and holes under visible light illumination. Photogenerated holes could directly react with NO to yield NO3 −, or it can react with hydroxyl to produce OH, which can further oxidize NO into NO2 and NO3 − as well. On the other hand, as GQDs was reported as an efficient electron transfer mediator, photogenerated electrons were first transferred to GQDs, this process effectively improve the charge separation efficiency of the system. Then, electrons on GQDs could reduce O2 in the system to O2 −, which could also directly oxidize NO into ionic species NO3 −. Similar reaction processes have been widely accepted for photocatalytic removal of NO [15,35] and detailed reactions are as follows:
(1)
SnO
2
/ GQDs + h ν →
e
-
+
h
+
(2)
3
h
+
+ NO + 2
H
2
O →
NO
3
-
+ 4
H
+
(3) e−+O2→ O2 −
(4) NO+ O2 −→NO3 −
(5) h++H2O→ OH+H+
(6) NO+2 OH→NO2+H2O
(7) NO2+ OH→NO3 −+H+

In summary, we have prepared a series of SnO2/GQDs composites via a facile method. The composites could function as an efficient photocatalyst for removal of indoor NO under visible light illumination. Among them, the composite with 1% GQDs exhibits the highest photocatalytic NO degradation ratio of 57% and excellent selectivity with NO2 generated fraction as low as 5%. The introduction of GQDs in the system has little influence on the structure and morphology of SnO2, but it notably improves the efficiency of light harvesting and charge separation of the system. This lead to the remarkable increase in the amount of active species in the system, which significantly enhance the efficiency for photocatalytic removal of NO in contrast to that of SnO2 alone. It is expected that this work could render a novel way for application in indoor air purification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21403172), the Innovative Research Team of Sichuan Province (2016TD0011), Sichuan Provincial Education Department Project (15ZB0055), the Scientific Research Starting Project of SWPU (2015QHZ018) and Young Scholars Development Fund of SWPU (201499010096).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.04.145.
